children.name: Daphoenositta
children.description: A genus of small, nuthatch-like passerines endemic to the Australo-Papuan region, named for their tree-climbing habits that superficially resemble the Northern Hemisphere nuthatches. They move headfirst down tree trunks and along branches, using their strong legs and sharp claws to maintain a firm grip on bark. Their plumage is typically a combination of grey, black, and rufous.

children.name: Daphoenositta chrysoptera
children.description: A small, active tree-climber with grey upperparts, a black-and-white head pattern, and a bright rufous-and-black wing patch, found across Australia in wooded habitats. It forages in small, noisy flocks, creeping along branches and trunks in all orientations, gleaning insects from bark crevices. Its distinctive, piping contact calls keep the flock in constant communication.

children.name: Daphoenositta miranda
children.description: A striking black sittella with a pale yellow bill and a prominent white patch on the wing, found in the montane forests of New Guinea. It forages in the same nuthatch-like manner as its Australian relatives, creeping along moss-covered branches in the misty cloud forests. Little is known of its breeding biology due to the remoteness of its highland habitat.

children.name: Daphoenositta papuensis
children.description: A small sittella with rufous underparts, a black cap, and a white face, found in the lowland and hill forests of New Guinea. It behaves like a nuthatch, creeping headfirst down trunks and along branches in search of bark-dwelling insects. It is typically seen in small, active groups, often accompanying mixed-species foraging flocks.
